name: Corpse Candle
description: Corpse Candles, also known as corpse lights or will-o'-the-wisps, are spectral phenomena deeply rooted in Scandinavian folklore, particularly within Norse traditions. These eerie lights are believed to manifest near graveyards, bogs, and other liminal spaces associated with death and the supernatural. They are often interpreted as the restless spirits of the deceased, trapped between worlds or attempting to guide the living towards hidden treasures, lost paths, or, more ominously, their own demise. The appearance of a corpse candle is often seen as an omen, portending death or misfortune within the community. Their flickering, ethereal glow serves as a chilling reminder of mortality and the ever-present presence of the spirit world, blurring the line between the realms of the living and the dead. These spectral flames are not benevolent guides; they are often mischievous or malevolent, leading travelers astray and luring them into dangerous situations. The corpse candle embodies the Norse fear of the dead and the unpredictable nature of the supernatural.
subtitle: Spectral lights of Norse folklore, associated with death and misfortune.
appearance: Corpse candles typically appear as flickering, ethereal lights, often described as blue, green, or white in color. They may resemble small flames, orbs of light, or even human-like shapes from a distance. Their luminosity can vary, sometimes appearing bright and intense, while at other times dim and almost imperceptible. They often move erratically, darting back and forth or floating just above the ground, creating a disorienting and unsettling effect.
habitat: Corpse candles are most commonly found in graveyards, bogs, swamps, and other desolate or liminal spaces associated with death and the supernatural. They are also said to appear near unmarked graves or sites of tragic deaths.
behavior: Corpse candles are generally considered to be mischievous or malevolent entities. They often lure travelers off course, leading them into dangerous terrain or towards their own demise. Some legends suggest they are guarding hidden treasures, while others claim they are simply trapped spirits seeking to cause harm or chaos. Their behavior is unpredictable, and their motives remain shrouded in mystery.

keyMyths.title: The Lost Traveler
keyMyths.description: A lone traveler, lost in the Scandinavian wilderness, followed a corpse candle in hopes of finding his way back to civilization. The light led him deeper and deeper into the forest, eventually leading him to a treacherous bog where he perished. The corpse candle was said to be the spirit of a man who had died in the bog years before, seeking to claim another victim.

keyMyths.title: The Hidden Treasure
keyMyths.description: A group of villagers noticed a corpse candle flickering near an old burial mound. They believed it was guarding a hidden treasure and attempted to dig up the mound. However, as they dug, the corpse candle grew brighter and brighter, until it exploded in a blinding flash, scaring the villagers away and leaving them empty-handed. The treasure remained hidden, guarded by the restless spirit.

keyMyths.title: The Omen of Death
keyMyths.description: A family saw a corpse candle flickering outside their home one night. They took it as a sign of impending death, and within a week, the family patriarch passed away. The corpse candle was seen as a harbinger of doom, a chilling reminder of the fragility of life and the inevitability of death.

sources.author: Davidson, H.R. Ellis
sources.work: Gods and Myths of Northern Europe
sources.description: Provides a comprehensive overview of Norse mythology and folklore, including discussions of spirits and supernatural phenomena.
